title: Change Risk Assessment Accuracy
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:change_request" state="Closed"
| eval predicted=case(match(lower(u_risk),"low"),"Low",match(lower(u_risk),"medium"),"Medium",match(lower(u_risk),"high"),"High",true(),"Unknown")
| eval actual=if(lower(close_code)="unsuccessful" OR lower(u_customer_impact)="yes","Bad","Good")
| stats count by predicted, actual
| eventstats sum(count) as tot by predicted
| eval pct=round(100*count/tot,1)
| where predicted="Low" AND actual="Bad"
description: If “low risk” changes often fail or drive incidents, the risk model is miscalibrated; analytics improve scoring and reduce surprise outages.
value: If “low risk” changes often fail or drive incidents, the risk model is miscalibrated; analytics improve scoring and reduce surprise outages.
implementation: Compare `u_risk` at submission to outcomes (`close_code`, customer impact, related incident count within 24h). Build confusion-style matrix: predicted risk vs actual. Quarterly review with change managers; adjust questionnaires and automation gates. Requires consistent field extraction in the TA.
visualization: Matrix heatmap (predicted vs actual), Table (low-risk failures), Line chart (calibration trend quarter over quarter).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_snow`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=snow:change_request`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Compare `u_risk` at submission to outcomes (`close_code`, customer impact, related incident count within 24h). Build confusion-style matrix: predicted risk vs actual. Quarterly review with change managers; adjust questionnaires and automation gates. Requires consistent field extraction in the TA.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:change_request" state="Closed"
| eval predicted=case(match(lower(u_risk),"low"),"Low",match(lower(u_risk),"medium"),"Medium",match(lower(u_risk),"high"),"High",true(),"Unknown")
| eval actual=if(lower(close_code)="unsuccessful" OR lower(u_customer_impact)="yes","Bad","Good")
| stats count by predicted, actual
| eventstats sum(count) as tot by predicted
| eval pct=round(100*count/tot,1)
| where predicted="Low" AND actual="Bad"
```

Understanding this SPL

**Change Risk Assessment Accuracy** — If “low risk” changes often fail or drive incidents, the risk model is miscalibrated; analytics improve scoring and reduce surprise outages.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=snow:change_request`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_snow`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:change_request. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:change_request". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **predicted** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **actual** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by predicted, actual** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by predicted** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where predicted="Low" AND actual="Bad"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Matrix heatmap (predicted vs actual), Table (low-risk failures), Line chart (calibration trend quarter over quarter).
grandmaExplanation: If “low risk” changes often fail or drive incidents, the risk model is miscalibrated; analytics improve scoring and reduce surprise outages — so tickets, changes, and incidents are on track and nothing is silently missed.
